critic_raw:
This is a well-structured experimental design with several good instincts: prospective labeling, train-only preprocessing, stratified splitting, and explicit scope limitations. However, there are two genuine methodological problems worth naming precisely.

**Issue 1: The non-overlapping window claim is incomplete with respect to temporal leakage from the sequence model's input.**

The design states labels use non-overlapping 7-day prediction windows (preventing artificial label correlation), and splits are done at the 'window level.' But the sequence model uses a 7-day rolling input window of sensor data ending at time t to predict failures in [t, t+7]. If windows are partitioned with asset-stratified sampling *across the full observation period* — meaning a window from asset A on day 200 can be in training while a window from asset A on day 207 is in validation — then the raw sensor sequences for adjacent windows overlap substantially (up to 6/7 of the timesteps are shared). This is not label leakage, but it is feature leakage: the model's input for a test window may be highly correlated with inputs for training windows that temporally overlap. More critically, for assets experiencing a slowly evolving fault, the model may have seen the middle portion of a fault trajectory in training and predicts the end of it in test. This inflates measured AUROC because the sequence model is pattern-matching on waveform segments it has partially observed, not generalizing to future fault states. The tabular baseline is less exposed because it aggregates away this temporal texture.

Evidence one way or the other: compare performance under asset-stratified random split (current design) vs. a strict temporal split (all windows before month 30 in train, month 30–33 in val, month 33–36 in test, applied per asset). If AUROC degrades substantially under the temporal split, the feature-level leakage mechanism is operative. This is a standard robustness check for time-series classification that is absent here.

**Issue 2: The asset-stratified split is argued as a confound control, but it is actually the mechanism for the leakage in Issue 1 and introduces a specific additional confound.**

Having all machines appear in train and test is described as controlling for per-asset confounding. But for predictive maintenance specifically, the model can learn asset-specific signatures — not just failure physics, but the characteristic 'fingerprint' of each machine's nominal operation. When that same machine appears in the test set, the model has an unfair advantage over any deployment scenario where it would encounter a machine not in its training history. The per-asset AUROC stratification proposed in Section 6 will not detect this because it measures performance conditional on machine identity being known at train time, which is the exact condition that enables the artifact. The claimed confound control (all machines in train+test) is structurally identical to the confound it is supposed to control.

Evidence one way or the other: hold out 3–5 machines entirely from training and evaluate on those machines only. If AUROC drops sharply, the model is exploiting asset identity signatures rather than generalizable failure precursors. This is not claimed as in-scope by Section 7, but it is necessary to interpret whether the in-scope result (same assets) reflects a real temporal-signal advantage.

**Issue 3 (secondary): Label asymmetry between the two model types under overlapping failure proximity.**

The 7-day label window is binary: any unplanned downtime with root cause in [t, t+7]. For assets with multiple failures, windows at t and t+1 both receive label=1 if the same failure event falls in both windows. The design says 'non-overlapping windows prevent artificial correlation' — but this applies to the *prediction windows*, not to whether the same failure event can label multiple consecutive windows. If daily windows are used with a 7-day label horizon, a single failure event labels up to 7 consecutive windows as positive. Whether these 7 windows are non-overlapping with each other or are all positives depends on the windowing scheme, which is not fully specified. If consecutive 1-day-step windows are used (which 'daily prediction window' implies), the same failure can label 7 adjacent windows, and because the sequence model sees the trailing sensor signal in each, earlier positive windows see less of the pre-failure signal — creating a non-uniform signal distribution within the positive class. This matters because AUROC averages across all positive windows equally, but they have structurally different label-to-signal distances. The tabular baseline is less sensitive to this because it collapses the time dimension anyway.

Evidence one way or the other: report the distribution of 'days-to-failure' within positive windows (i.e., for each positive window, how many days until the actual failure event). If positive windows are heavily concentrated near failure (days 1–2 before the event), the AUROC reflects a harder version of the problem; if they are distributed across all 7 days, the model must genuinely generalize across the full horizon, and the effective sample size of 'difficult' positive windows may be much smaller than the nominal ≥100 target.

**What this design gets right:** Prospective label construction before model development, train-only preprocessing, explicit scope limitation to same-machine generalization, and the calibration check are all sound. The confound check proposed in Section 6 (inspecting whether gains concentrate near scheduled maintenance) is a good idea, even if it cannot be done analytically in advance.

**Summary:** The dominant flaw is the asset-stratified random split that allows temporal overlap of sequence inputs across partitions, making it structurally impossible to distinguish 'the model learned temporal dynamics predictive of failure' from 'the model pattern-matched on partially-observed fault trajectories it saw in training.' The fix — a temporal holdout split — is standard and feasible with 36 months of data.
all_issues_raised: Asset-stratified random splits allow the sequence model's input windows to temporally overlap between train and test, enabling partial fault-trajectory memorization rather than genuine temporal generalization.

Having all machines in both train and test means the model can exploit asset-specific nominal signatures; the proposed per-asset stratification cannot detect this because it conditions on machine identity being known at train time.

Daily sliding prediction windows with a 7-day label horizon can assign the same failure event to up to 7 consecutive positive windows, creating non-uniform signal-to-label distances within the positive class that AUROC does not account for.
all_issues_adjudicated: Asset-stratified random splits allow the sequence model's input windows to temporally overlap between train and test, enabling partial fault-trajectory memorization rather than genuine temporal generalization.

Having all machines in both train and test means the model can exploit asset-specific nominal signatures; the proposed per-asset stratification cannot detect this because it conditions on machine identity being known at train time.

Daily sliding prediction windows with a 7-day label horizon can assign the same failure event to up to 7 consecutive positive windows, creating non-uniform signal-to-label distances within the positive class that AUROC does not account for.
